questions.stem: A 65-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 4-hour history of palpitations. He feels lightheaded but has no chest pain or breathlessness. Pulse is irregularly irregular at 110 bpm. Blood pressure 125/78 mmHg. ECG shows absence of P waves and an irregularly irregular ventricular rate. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The ECG shows irregularly irregular rhythm without P waves, classic for atrial fibrillation. Atrial flutter typically has sawtooth waves and regular ventricular response unless variable block, but then the irregularity is not as chaotic. Multifocal atrial tachycardia requires at least three distinct P wave morphologies. Sinus arrhythmia is a regular variation with respiration.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old woman with no prior cardiac history is brought to the emergency department with sudden onset breathlessness and central chest discomfort. On examination, she is pale, clammy, respiratory rate 28, oxygen saturation 91% on room air, blood pressure 84/52 mmHg, pulse 155 bpm and irregularly irregular, JVP elevated to 10 cm, and bibasal crackles. ECG shows atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response causing acute heart failure
Acute coronary syndrome with developing heart block
questions.explanation: The combination of irregularly irregular pulse, absent P waves, and rapid ventricular rate confirms atrial fibrillation. The haemodynamic compromise and pulmonary oedema are due to the rapid rate causing diastolic dysfunction and acute heart failure. Atrial flutter with 1:1 conduction is rare and usually has a regular rhythm. Ventricular tachycardia has broad QRS and haemodynamic instability but rhythm is typically regular.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man attends a preoperative assessment for elective hip replacement. He is asymptomatic, but his pulse is irregularly irregular at a rate of 78 bpm. He has a history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes. ECG shows no P waves and an irregular ventricular response. He is haemodynamically stable. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia in sinus rhythm
Sick sinus syndrome with tachy-brady pattern
questions.explanation: The incidental finding of irregularly irregular rhythm with absent P waves on ECG confirms atrial fibrillation. The patient is asymptomatic, so this is asymptomatic AF. Atrial flutter would have a regular atrial rate and often sawtooth waves. Sick sinus syndrome might have alternating bradycardia and tachycardia but ECG shows sinus pauses. Frequent atrial ectopics would have isolated premature P waves, not persistent irregularity.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman with a history of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation (not on anticoagulation) presents with acute onset right-sided weakness and dysarthria. CT head shows a wedge-shaped hypodensity in the left middle cerebral artery territory. She has no headache or neck stiffness. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Cardioembolic ischaemic stroke due to atrial fibrillation
questions.explanation: The wedge-shaped cortical hypodensity on CT indicates a territorial ischaemic infarct, most commonly cardioembolic in a patient with AF. Lacunar infarcts are small subcortical. Haemorrhagic transformation would have hyperdensity. Subarachnoid haemorrhage presents with thunderclap headache and blood on CT. TIA would have no infarct on CT.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with a 2-week history of palpitations, heat intolerance, and weight loss despite increased appetite. On examination, she has a fine tremor, warm moist skin, and a palpable goiter. Pulse is irregularly irregular at 120 bpm. ECG shows atrial fibrillation. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Atrial fibrillation due to ischaemic heart disease
Atrial fibrillation due to mitral valve prolapse
questions.explanation: The clinical picture of hyperthyroidism (goiter, tremor, weight loss, heat intolerance) with new-onset atrial fibrillation strongly suggests thyrotoxic AF. In a young patient without structural heart disease, hyperthyroidism is a common cause. Other options are less likely given the absence of signs.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man presents with a 2-day history of palpitations and dyspnoea. His pulse is irregularly irregular. What is the single most appropriate investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: A 12-lead ECG is the first-line test to confirm atrial fibrillation, showing absent P waves and an irregularly irregular rhythm. Holter is reserved for paroxysmal cases when resting ECG is normal.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman has episodes of palpitations lasting a few minutes, occurring about once a week. Her resting 12-lead ECG between episodes is normal. What is the most appropriate next investigation to diagnose the cause of her symptoms?
questions.explanation: For infrequent symptoms (<1 per day), an event recorder worn for weeks is more likely to capture an episode during symptoms than a 24-hour Holter, in line with NICE guidance for paroxysmal AF.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman with hypertension and diabetes presents with palpitations. An ECG shows atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 110 bpm. What is the most appropriate additional investigation to assess for underlying structural heart disease as a possible cause of her AF?
questions.explanation: Transthoracic echocardiography is the first-line imaging modality to assess cardiac structure and function in new-onset atrial fibrillation, as per NICE guidance, to identify valvular disease, left atrial size, and ventricular function.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man presents with palpitations. ECG shows an irregular rhythm with a ventricular rate of 100 bpm. There are clear flutter waves seen in leads II, III, and aVF. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Atrial flutter with variable AV block
questions.explanation: The presence of distinct flutter waves (sawtooth pattern in II, III, aVF) indicates atrial flutter; variable AV block results in an irregular ventricular rate, mimicking AF. Absent P waves in AF distinguishes it from flutter.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man presents with palpitations and breathlessness for 2 days. ECG shows atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 130 bpm. He is haemodynamically stable with a blood pressure of 135/85 mmHg. He has no significant past medical history and no structural heart disease. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for rate control?
questions.explanation: NICE recommends a beta-blocker (e.g., bisoprolol) as first-line rate control for AF without heart failure. Digoxin is second-line; verapamil is an alternative but not first-line. Amiodarone and flecainide are rhythm control agents, not for rate control.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old female presents with acute onset palpitations, chest pain, and hypotension (BP 85/50 mmHg). ECG shows atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 180 bpm. She is drowsy. Which is the most appropriate immediate management?
questions.explanation: This patient has haemodynamic instability due to rapid AF. Synchronised DC cardioversion is the treatment of choice. Drug therapies have slower onset and are not adequate in this emergency.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with paroxysmal atrial fibrillation (AF) has infrequent symptomatic episodes (2-3 per year). He has no structural heart disease, normal left ventricular function, and no ischaemic heart disease. During an episode, his heart rate is 110 bpm and BP is 130/80 mmHg. What is the most appropriate management for acute episodes?
questions.explanation: For patients with infrequent symptomatic paroxysmal AF and no structural heart disease, NICE recommends a 'pill-in-the-pocket' approach with flecainide. Beta-blockers are less effective for conversion; digoxin has no role; warfarin is for stroke prevention, not rhythm control.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old woman with permanent atrial fibrillation has a CHA2DS2-VASc score of 4 (age 80, hypertension, diabetes). She has no contraindications to anticoagulation. What is the most appropriate therapy for stroke prevention?
questions.explanation: NICE recommends a DOAC (e.g., apixaban) as first-line over warfarin for stroke prevention in AF due to better safety and efficacy. Aspirin and clopidogrel are not recommended (NICE 2021). High CHA2DS2-VASc score mandates anticoagulation.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old man with known Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) syndrome presents with palpitations and light-headedness. ECG shows atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 200 bpm and a broad complex (pre-excited) pattern. Blood pressure is 100/70 mmHg. Which of the following treatments is CONTRAINDICATED in this patient?
questions.explanation: In pre-excited AF, AV nodal blocking drugs such as digoxin (and verapamil, beta-blockers) are contraindicated because they can increase conduction via the accessory pathway, risking ventricular fibrillation. Flecainide, amiodarone, and procainamide slow accessory pathway conduction; DC cardioversion is safe.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man presents to the ED with palpitations for 6 hours. He is haemodynamically stable with a blood pressure of 130/80 mmHg. ECG shows atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 140 bpm. He has no chest pain or heart failure. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Start oral bisoprolol for heart rate control
questions.explanation: In haemodynamically stable AF with fast ventricular rate, NICE recommends rate control with a beta blocker (e.g., bisoprolol) as first line. Digoxin is second line, amiodarone is for rhythm control, cardioversion is not indicated without instability, and anticoagulation is not the immediate priority.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman with hypertension and well-controlled paroxysmal atrial fibrillation is reviewed in clinic. She is asymptomatic, with a CHA2DS2-VASc score of 3 (age 65, hypertension, female sex). She is on no antithrombotic therapy. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Start warfarin with a target INR of 2-3
Start bisoprolol 2.5 mg daily for rate control
questions.explanation: With a CHA2DS2-VASc score of 3, the patient meets criteria for anticoagulation. NICE recommends a DOAC (e.g., apixaban) over warfarin as first line. Rate or rhythm control is not indicated in asymptomatic paroxysmal AF.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old man with known hypertension and diabetes presents with acute onset palpitations and confusion. His heart rate is 170 bpm, blood pressure 85/50 mmHg, and he has signs of pulmonary oedema. ECG shows atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Give a 500 mL bolus of normal saline
questions.explanation: Haemodynamic instability (hypotension, pulmonary oedema) in rapid AF mandates immediate synchronised DC cardioversion. Rate control with beta blockers or digoxin is too slow, and IV amiodarone is reserved for stable patients or after cardioversion.

questions.stem: An 82-year-old woman with chronic atrial fibrillation on warfarin (INR 3.0) presents to the ED with acute onset right-sided weakness and aphasia. Onset was 2 hours ago. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Give intravenous vitamin K to reverse warfarin
Perform a CT angiogram of the head and neck
questions.explanation: In a patient with AF on anticoagulation presenting with acute stroke, urgent non-contrast CT head is essential to differentiate ischaemic from haemorrhagic stroke. Thrombolysis is contraindicated with INR >1.7, and reversing anticoagulation is only indicated if haemorrhage is confirmed.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old man with permanent atrial fibrillation and chronic kidney disease stage 4 (eGFR 25 ml/min/1.73m²) is reviewed. His CHA2DS2-VASc score is 5 (heart failure, hypertension, age >75, diabetes). He is not on anticoagulation. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Start warfarin with a target INR of 2-3
questions.explanation: In AF with CKD stage 4, NICE recommends a DOAC with dose adjustment. Apixaban 2.5 mg BD is licensed for CrCl 15-29 ml/min. Warfarin is an alternative but DOAC is preferred. Standard dose apixaban (5 mg BD) is not appropriate, and rivaroxaban is not recommended at this CrCl (caution).

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman presents with sudden onset palpitations and breathlessness. Her heart rate is 150 bpm, irregularly irregular. Blood pressure is 85/50 mmHg. Which single finding indicates this is a medical emergency requiring immediate intervention?
questions.explanation: A systolic BP < 90 mmHg indicates haemodynamic instability, which mandates emergency cardioversion. While a fast rate and breathlessness are concerning, the BP threshold defines the need for immediate action.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with no prior cardiac history presents with central chest pain for 2 hours. ECG shows atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 110 bpm. Which finding most strongly suggests an acute coronary syndrome as the cause of his arrhythmia?
options: 2 mm ST elevation in leads II, III, aVF
questions.explanation: ST elevation in an inferior distribution indicates acute ST-elevation myocardial infarction, a life-threatening cause of AF. The other findings are less specific or not acute.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man presents with palpitations and presyncope. ECG shows an irregular tachycardia with broad QRS complexes. Which finding is most characteristic of pre-excited atrial fibrillation?
options: Rate of 150 bpm with fixed QRS width
Delta waves seen in all leads
Irregularly irregular rhythm with varying QRS morphology
Narrow QRS complexes with irregular rhythm
questions.explanation: In pre-excited AF, the accessory pathway conducts rapidly, producing an irregularly irregular wide complex tachycardia with varying QRS morphology due to fusion. This is a can't-miss because it can degenerate to VF.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old woman on warfarin for atrial fibrillation (INR 3.2) presents with sudden severe headache and vomiting. Which clinical finding most strongly suggests an intracranial haemorrhage?
options: Glasgow Coma Scale 12/15 with right-sided weakness
Conjugate eye deviation to the left
questions.explanation: A reduced GCS with a focal motor deficit in an anticoagulated patient is highly suspicious for intracranial haemorrhage, requiring urgent CT. Isolated hypertension or papilloedema are less specific in this context.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with atrial fibrillation presents with acute pleuritic chest pain and dyspnoea. Her heart rate is 105 bpm irregularly irregular. Which ECG finding raises concern for pulmonary embolism?
options: Flattened T waves in all leads
Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
questions.explanation: The S1Q3T3 pattern (S wave in I, Q wave and T wave inversion in III) is classic for acute right heart strain due to PE. While AF can be secondary to PE, this specific finding should prompt urgent investigation.
